title: Hardest-impostor margin hinge, retested on the confusability-weighted champion
hypothesis: The §6 metric is bound by ranking quality, not descriptor content or the operating point: iter-12 probes of the exp-25 champion (runs/_probe_iter12_field.py) found the true cell is the field's top-1 on only 2% of eval crops while making the top-20 (of 1024) on 33-58% in every lighting bucket, with decode, rotation, and operating-point mechanisms all ruled out as the wall (see project memory champion-failure-mode-probes). Exp 35 (kept, 797.91 -> 743.07 m) improved this by changing WHICH locations get training exposure (confusability-weighted sampling over cell-descriptor lookalikes) but never touched the training SIGNAL itself: loss_fn's Gaussian-smeared CE (sigma=1.5 cells) still spreads its wrong-cell gradient across all 1024 cells in proportion to softmax mass, so no gradient concentrates specifically on the one distant lookalike currently outranking the truth -- exactly the objective-family gap exp 34 pre-registered (hardest-impostor margin hinge) and never got to test: its run died with ENOSPC inside prepare_realizations before a single gradient step (timings.json score_s: 0, train_wall_s: 268 -- see project memory exp34-enospc-disk-lifecycle). The hypothesis is UNTESTED, not refuted, and the mechanism it targets (the training signal) is a materially different lever from exp 35's (the sampler). The two should compound rather than compete: exp 35 already brings confusable cell pairs to the model more often; the margin hinge is the loss term that actually forces their scores apart once they arrive, rather than relying on CE's diffuse likelihood gradient to do it incidentally. Disk is currently clean (33 GB free per df -h) so this run should not repeat exp 34's infrastructure failure.
method: ONE change, entirely inside model/model.py's loss_fn (training-only; forward(), export_onnx(), estimate_position(), and all of train.py -- sampler, prepare_realizations, schedule, calibrate_conf_shift -- are untouched): add a hardest-impostor margin hinge on the same C4-voted logits the decode reads. Per crop: s_pos = max logit within Chebyshev radius 1 of the true cell (the 3x3 block a 128 m crop legitimately straddles across a ~220 m cell boundary); s_neg = max logit over cells at Chebyshev radius > 3 (outside the 7x7 ring the sigma=1.5-cell Gaussian CE bump already covers, so the hinge never fights the CE term over near-misses -- same exclusion radius convention train.py's compute_cell_weights already uses for its confusability lookup, NEIGHBOR_EXCLUDE_R=1, just applied at a wider ring for the negative). loss += 0.5 * relu(1.0 + s_neg - s_pos), added to loss_fn's existing returned sum (ce + coord_loss + 0.3 * conf_loss). Margin 1.0 logit is roughly a 20x mass ratio after the beta=3 decode sharpening already in forward(). CE, coord L2, conf BCE, the sampler, the epoch schedule, and the calibration procedure are all unchanged, so the deployment gates and operating-point comparison to the champion stay apples-to-apples.
expected_outcome: Worst-case median kept error improves from the champion's 743.07 m to roughly 550-700 m, with the improvement spread across area x bucket cells rather than concentrated in one -- a real ranking fix, not an operating-point artifact, since the calibration procedure is unchanged and coverage should stay comparable to the champion's (see coverage-median-coupling memory: any large coverage shift would make the comparison unmeasured, not a genuine win). Mechanism-level prediction, matched to the iter-12 probe protocol: true-cell top-1 rate should rise measurably above the champion's ~2% baseline, and median sharpened peak mass should rise above ~0.14. Falsification fork: if primary metric doesn't improve even though ranking/peak-mass probes do, the wall has moved from the objective to descriptor information content itself -- pointing future iterations back at the trunk/feature capacity rather than the loss or sampler.
eli5: Right now the model's best two guesses for ‘where is this’ are often a real place and a look-alike place somewhere else on the map, and it only barely favors the real one. Last round we made it practice more on exactly those confusing pairs; this round we also change how hard it gets corrected when it still ranks a look-alike above the truth. Instead of a gentle nudge spread thinly over the whole map, it now gets a direct penalty aimed specifically at its single worst rival guess, every time, until the true spot clearly wins.

stages.name: Camera frame
stages.detail: 128×128 px aerial crop, one of 6 simulated lighting conditions

stages.name: Rotation fan
stages.detail: the crop is copied into its four 90° turns (exact pixel re-arrangements, no blur) and all four copies travel through the same network side by side

stages.name: Feature extractor
stages.detail: ImageNet-pretrained MobileNetV3-Small (first 9 blocks, 190k params), gently fine-tuned

stages.name: Layout summary
stages.detail: a slim extra layer condenses the 8×8 feature grid into a 512-number code that remembers what sits where in the crop; the texture average (48 numbers) is kept alongside

stages.name: Lighting gate
stages.detail: a tiny network reads the crop's own average brightness together with the 48-number texture summary and outputs one number: how night-like is this shot?

stages.name: Probability map
stages.detail: two scorers read each of the four turned copies and the gate blends their per-cell guesses; the four resulting heat maps are averaged into one voted map — a location only stays hot if it wins from every direction

stages.name: Decode
stages.detail: the heat map's contrast is turned up first (peaks boosted, faint background suppressed), then the answer is the balance point of the sharpened map — committing to the strongest hotspot instead of averaging every faint guess across the whole city

stages.name: Confidence
stages.detail: the model inspects the shape of its own heat map — one crisp hotspot means sure, a washed-out smear means unsure — and below a tuned bar it says 'no fix' instead of guessing; the bar is set so it still answers at least 40% of the time in every lighting condition

stages.name: Training data
stages.detail: every pass still draws a fresh 6,000 places per bucket, but weighted: places whose map tile looks like some far-off tile are drawn roughly twice as often as one-of-a-kind places, so confusing pairs get extra practice

stages.name: Training signal
stages.detail: the usual grading (reward probability near the true spot) now adds a direct penalty: whenever some distant look-alike cell's score still beats the true cell's score by less than a safety margin, that one worst impostor is pushed down until it clears the margin — a targeted correction instead of a penalty smeared over the whole map

stages.name: Training schedule
stages.detail: unchanged — 24 fresh-draw passes with the cosine learning-rate glide kept from round 25

implementation_brief:
Edit ONLY model/model.py's loss_fn. Do not touch forward(), export_onnx(), estimate_position(), build_model(), TinyLocNet, or any file in model/train.py -- the frozen contracts are: train.py's CLI (python -m model.train --area <area> --out-dir <dir> [--epochs N]) and model.py's ONNX export contract (1x3x128x128 float32 [0,1] input -> [[u, v, conf]] output), both restated in model.py's module docstring; neither changes here.

1. Add two module-level constants near the existing GRID_K/TARGET_SIGMA_CELLS/DECODE_BETA block: `MARGIN_ALPHA = 0.5` (hinge loss weight) and `MARGIN_MARGIN = 1.0` (target logit gap).

2. Add a small cached-by-device helper, e.g. `_MARGIN_MASKS = {}` module-level dict, and a function `_margin_masks(k, device)` that builds and caches (keyed by (k, str(device))) two [k*k, k*k] boolean tensors on first use: `pos_mask[i,j] = True` if Chebyshev(cell i, cell j) <= 1, and `neg_mask[i,j] = True` if Chebyshev(cell i, cell j) > 3. Build via the same gy,gx = divmod(arange(k*k), k) and cheb = max(|dx|,|dy|) construction train.py's compute_cell_weights already uses for its own confusability matrix (model/train.py, function compute_cell_weights) -- reuse that exact index convention (flat index = gy*k + gx) so the true cell's neighborhood lookup is consistent with every other place in the codebase that indexes this grid (_grid_centers in this same file, compute_cell_weights in train.py).

3. Inside loss_fn(pred, logits, target_uv), after the existing `ce` computation and before the final return, add:
   - `k = GRID_K`
   - the true flat cell index per crop: `true_gx = (target_uv[:, 0] * k).long().clamp(0, k - 1)`, `true_gy = (target_uv[:, 1] * k).long().clamp(0, k - 1)`, `true_idx = true_gy * k + true_gx` (matches the gy*k+gx convention above -- this is reading target_uv's existing (u, v) axis convention, not introducing a new one; sanity-check against _grid_centers if unsure which of target_uv[:,0]/[:,1] maps to gx vs gy).
   - `pos_mask, neg_mask = _margin_masks(k, logits.device)`
   - `pos_row = pos_mask[true_idx]` and `neg_row = neg_mask[true_idx]`, both [N, k*k] bool via advanced indexing.
   - `s_pos = logits.masked_fill(~pos_row, float('-inf')).amax(dim=1)`
   - `s_neg = logits.masked_fill(~neg_row, float('-inf')).amax(dim=1)`
   - `margin_loss = torch.relu(MARGIN_MARGIN + s_neg - s_pos).mean()`
   - change the function's final line from `return ce + coord_loss + 0.3 * conf_loss` to `return ce + coord_loss + 0.3 * conf_loss + MARGIN_ALPHA * margin_loss`.

4. Leave the Gaussian-bump CE (`g`, `ce`), `coord_loss`, and `conf_loss` byte-for-byte identical to today -- this is strictly an additive term. Leave GRID_K, TARGET_SIGMA_CELLS, DECODE_BETA, GOOD_ERR_UV, and every other constant untouched.

5. train.py needs no edits: loss_fn's call site in train_area's training loop already passes (out, logits, yb) positionally and doesn't inspect the loss's internal composition. If convenient, log MARGIN_ALPHA and MARGIN_MARGIN into train.py's `info` dict (train_area, near the existing `confusability_alpha` entry) for lineage -- but this is optional polish, not required for the experiment to run correctly.

Sanity check before finishing: `pos_mask` and `neg_mask` must be disjoint from each other for every row (Chebyshev <=1 vs >3 never overlap by construction) and neither should ever be all-False for a valid true_idx (every cell has itself in radius <=1, and every cell has cells at radius >3 unless k is very small -- GRID_K=32 here, so this holds trivially).
